slide_006.appears_in: Separation of concerns Rigor and formality Separation of concerns Modularity Abstraction Anticipation of change Generality Incremental Development To dominate the complexity, separate the issues to concentrate on one at a time "Divide & conquer" (divide et impera) Supports parallelization/synchronisations of efforts and separation of responsibilities

slide_010.appears_in: Mn should be highly cohesive module Mi is a "self-contained" meaningful unit, a system itself, which is a subsystem of a given system S. slide_011.appears_in: On the contrary, Modules should exhibit low coupling modules should have low interactions with others (otherwise, they cannot be designed on their own, e.g. abstraction from the other modules and the connectivity would not work)

slide_013.appears_in: Abstraction Rigor and formality Separation of concerns Modularity Abstraction Anticipation of change Generality Incremental Development Identify the important aspects of a phenomenon and ignore its details Linked closely to separation of concerns Type of abstraction to apply depends on purpose Example: the user interface of a watch (its buttons) abstracts from the watch's internals for the purpose of setting time.

slide_014.appears_in: Abstraction: we have done it already many times!

You make your own abstraction introducing the context of the system, You make your abstractions when prioritise requirements You make your abstractions when you define data types, i.e. defining the precision

slide_017.appears_in: Incremental development <sup>l</sup> Rather than deliver the system in a single attempt, the development and delivery is broken down into increments with each increment delivering part of the required functionality <sup>l</sup>

Incremental development <sup>l</sup> Rather than deliver the system in a single attempt, the development and delivery is broken down into increments with each increment delivering part of the required functionality <sup>l</sup>

slide_019.appears_in: Incremental development advantages <sup>l</sup> Customer value can be delivered with each increment so system functionality is available earlier <sup>l</sup>
Early increments act as a prototype to help elicit requirements for later increments <sup>l</sup>
